subject: mathematics

sections.title: Curious about Zero
sections.think_reflect: Whether the absence of a recorded zero in a number system would limit the kinds of calculations and place-value notation that system could express.

sections.title: Integers
sections.think_reflect: Why every whole number is an integer but every integer is not a whole number, and what the negative integers add that whole numbers alone cannot express.

sections.title: Rational Numbers
sections.think_reflect: Whether a number with a negative sign can still be called a rational number, and whether every integer can itself be written as a rational number.

sections.title: Standard Form of a Rational Number
Rational Numbers on the Number Line
Irrational Numbers
sections.think_reflect: Whether a number whose decimal expansion never terminates and never repeats can still be located exactly on the number line, and why assuming a rational root in the proof leads to a contradiction.

sections.title: Irrational Numbers on the Number Line

prose_summary: Section 3.1 introduces the historical origin of counting and number systems, tracing tally-mark evidence such as the Lebombo and Ishango bones and describing how ancient Indian civilisation developed verbal number names; subsection 3.1.1 narrates the birth of numbers from tallying to symbolic counting, and subsection 3.1.2 records the ancient Indian number names and their place-value logic going up to very large named quantities. Section 3.2 introduces zero as a number in its own right and explains its historical absence and eventual recognition; subsection 3.2.1 defines whole numbers as natural numbers together with zero and revisits their closure properties under addition and multiplication, and subsection 3.2.2 credits Brahmagupta's formal articulation of zero's arithmetic rules, including division by zero being undefined. Section 3.3 introduces integers as the extension of whole numbers to include negative counterparts and justifies why negative numbers are necessary for representing quantities such as debt, temperature below zero, and elevation below sea level; subsection 3.3.1 develops the need for negative numbers through such real-world contexts and situates integers on the number line. Section 3.4 introduces rational numbers as numbers expressible in the form p/q with q not equal to zero and distinguishes them from integers and whole numbers; subsection 3.4.1 explains and constructs the standard form of a rational number (denominator positive, no common factor other than 1), and subsection 3.4.2 represents rational numbers on the number line and demonstrates locating numbers between any two given rationals. Section 3.5 introduces irrational numbers as numbers that cannot be expressed as p/q and motivates their discovery through the diagonal of a unit square; subsection 3.5.1 narrates the historical discovery of irrational numbers including the Pythagorean encounter with √2, subsection 3.5.2 proves by contradiction that √2 is irrational through an eight-step deductive argument assuming a coprime rational form and deriving a contradiction in the parity of p and q, and subsection 3.5.3 constructs irrational numbers such as √2 and √3 on the number line using right-triangle and spiral constructions. Section 3.6 revisits the decimal expansion of numbers to classify them by termination and recurrence; subsection 3.6.1 explains and computes terminating and non-terminating recurring decimal expansions of rational numbers and converts recurring decimals back to p/q form, subsection 3.6.2 explains cyclic numbers through the example of 1/7 and its repeating-digit pattern under multiplication, and subsection 3.6.3 explains that irrational numbers have non-terminating, non-repeating decimal expansions and contrasts this with the rational case. Section 3.7 concludes the chapter by consolidating the number system's hierarchy from natural numbers through whole numbers, integers, rational numbers, and irrational numbers into the real numbers, and summarises the chapter's key definitions and results.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Express a given integer in the standard form of a rational number p/q.
Reduce a rational number with a negative denominator to its standard form.
Find a rational number lying between two given rational numbers.
Find several rational numbers lying between two given rational numbers.
Determine whether the decimal expansion of a given rational number terminates or recurs, by examining the prime factors of the denominator.
Convert a given terminating decimal into its p/q rational form.
Convert a given purely recurring decimal into its p/q rational form.
Convert a given mixed recurring decimal into its p/q rational form.
Examine the repeating-digit pattern produced by multiplying the cyclic number formed from 1/7 by integers 1 through 6.

enumerated_exercises.description: Identify which of the given numbers are whole numbers and which are natural numbers.
State whether the given statements about whole numbers and zero are true or false, with reasons.
Verify closure of whole numbers under addition and multiplication for given pairs of numbers.
Represent the given real-world quantities (temperature, elevation, debt) as integers.
Arrange the given integers in ascending or descending order.
State whether the given statements about integers and whole numbers are true or false, with reasons.
Mark the given integers on a number line.
Express the given numbers in the standard form of a rational number.
Determine whether the given numbers are rational, giving reasons.
Identify the numerator and denominator of the given rational numbers expressed in standard form.
Reduce the given rational numbers to standard form.
Compare the given pairs of rational numbers.
Find three rational numbers equivalent to a given rational number.
State whether the given statements about rational numbers are true or false, with reasons.
Classify the given list of numbers as natural numbers, whole numbers, integers, and/or rational numbers.
Represent the given rational numbers on a number line.
Find a rational number lying between the two given rational numbers.
Find five rational numbers lying between the two given rational numbers.
Find ten rational numbers lying between the two given rational numbers.
Determine whether the given pair of rational numbers has a finite or infinite number of rational numbers between them, with reasoning.
Construct and locate the given irrational number on a number line using a geometric construction.
State whether the given statements about irrational numbers are true or false, with reasons.
Classify the given list of numbers as rational or irrational.
Find an irrational number lying between the two given rational numbers.
Classify the given list of numbers into natural numbers, whole numbers, integers, rational numbers, and irrational numbers.
State whether the given statements about the number system are true or false, with reasons.
Express the given rational number in standard form.
Find a rational number between the two given rational numbers.
Find the given number of rational numbers between two given rational numbers.
Determine whether the decimal expansion of the given rational number terminates or recurs.
Convert the given terminating or recurring decimal into p/q form.
Prove that the given number is irrational.
Locate the given irrational number on a number line using a geometric construction.
Identify whether the given decimal expansion represents a rational or an irrational number.
Arrange the given set of rational and irrational numbers in ascending order.
Justify with reasoning whether a given numerical claim about rational or irrational numbers is correct.
Solve a multi-step contextual problem requiring classification and ordering across the real number system.
